rawFindings.title: Missing Content-Security-Policy Header
rawFindings.description: No CSP header found. This makes the application more susceptible to XSS attacks.

rawFindings.title: Missing X-Frame-Options Header
rawFindings.description: The X-Frame-Options header is missing, potentially allowing clickjacking attacks. Note: Target uses Cloudflare which may inject this header at the platform level.

rawFindings.title: Missing Referrer-Policy Header
rawFindings.description: No Referrer-Policy set. Sensitive data in URLs may leak via the Referer header. Note: Target uses Cloudflare which may inject this header at the platform level.

rawFindings.title: Missing Permissions-Policy Header
rawFindings.description: No Permissions-Policy header. Browser features like camera/microphone are not explicitly restricted.

rawFindings.title: Missing Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy Header
rawFindings.description: The Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header is missing. This header isolates the browsing context, preventing cross-origin attacks like Spectre.

rawFindings.title: Missing Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy Header
rawFindings.description: The Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy header is missing. This header ensures all cross-origin resources are loaded with CORS or CORP, enabling cross-origin isolation.

rawFindings.title: Missing Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy Header
rawFindings.description: The Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header is missing. This header prevents other origins from loading this resource, mitigating side-channel attacks.

rawFindings.title: Cookie "__Secure-next-auth.csrf-token" Weak SameSite Setting
rawFindings.description: The cookie "__Secure-next-auth.csrf-token" has SameSite=None, allowing cross-site usage.

rawFindings.title: Cookie "__Secure-next-auth.callback-url" Missing HttpOnly Flag
rawFindings.description: The cookie "__Secure-next-auth.callback-url" is accessible via JavaScript, increasing XSS impact.

rawFindings.title: Cookie "__Secure-next-auth.callback-url" Weak SameSite Setting
rawFindings.description: The cookie "__Secure-next-auth.callback-url" has SameSite=None, allowing cross-site usage.

rawFindings.title: 1 Third-Party Cookies Missing HttpOnly
rawFindings.description: 1 analytics/marketing cookies are accessible via JavaScript. These are third-party tracking cookies with limited security impact.

rawFindings.title: Sensitive Data in URL (password/secret)
rawFindings.description: A URL contains what appears to be password/secret data as a query parameter. This data may be logged in server logs, browser history, and proxy caches.

rawFindings.title: Missing Subresource Integrity (SRI)
rawFindings.description: Found 7 external resource(s) without integrity attributes on https://cal.com/. Without SRI, if the CDN or external host is compromised, an attacker can inject malicious code that the browser will execute without verification. Add integrity="sha384-..." and crossorigin="anonymous" attributes to all external script and stylesheet tags.
rawFindings.evidence:
External resources without SRI:
<script> https://ddwl4m2hdecbv.cloudfront.net/b/46DJ4HWPMG61/46DJ4HWPMG61.js.gz
<script> https://eu-assets.i.posthog.com/static/array.js
<script> https://static.ads-twitter.com/uwt.js
<script> https://www.dubcdn.com/analytics/script.js

rawFindings.title: Sensitive Paths in robots.txt
rawFindings.description: The robots.txt file contains Disallow entries referencing sensitive paths. While robots.txt does not enforce access control, it reveals the existence of admin panels, APIs, or internal endpoints that attackers can target directly.

response.bodySnippet:
User-agent: *
Disallow: /sandbox
Disallow: /api
Disallow: /settings
Disallow: /settings/my-account

# Specifically allow access to OG Image api, otherwise eg Twitter won't render these images.
Allow: /api/social/og/image
# Specifically allow access to api/app-store to allow search engines to fetch assets
Allow: /api/app-store/

rawFindings.title: Missing Rate Limiting on Authentication Endpoint
rawFindings.description: The authentication endpoint at /api/auth/session does not enforce rate limiting. An attacker can perform brute-force password attacks, credential stuffing, or account enumeration without being throttled.
rawFindings.evidence: Sent 15 rapid requests — all returned HTTP 200 with no rate-limit headers (X-RateLimit-*, Retry-After, etc.) and no 429 response.

rawFindings.title: Missing Rate Limiting on API Endpoint
rawFindings.description: The API endpoint at /api/geolocation does not enforce rate limiting. This may allow abuse, scraping, or resource exhaustion attacks.
rawFindings.evidence: Sent 15 rapid requests — all returned HTTP 200 with no rate-limit headers (X-RateLimit-*, Retry-After, etc.) and no 429 response.

rawFindings.title: OAuth missing state parameter on /api/auth/session
rawFindings.description: OAuth authorization endpoint accepts requests without a state parameter, enabling CSRF attacks on the OAuth flow.
rawFindings.evidence: Endpoint responded with 200 without requiring state parameter

rawFindings.title: OAuth PKCE not enforced on /api/auth/session
rawFindings.description: The OAuth authorization endpoint does not require PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange). Without PKCE, authorization codes are vulnerable to interception attacks, especially on mobile/native apps.
rawFindings.evidence: Endpoint accepted authorization request without code_challenge parameter (HTTP 200)

rawFindings.title: XML External Entity (XXE) Injection — parameter-entity
rawFindings.description: The endpoint accepts XML input and is vulnerable to XXE injection. Parameter entity XXE — uses % entity for blind detection. An attacker can read arbitrary files from the server, perform SSRF, or cause denial of service.
rawFindings.evidence:
Technique: parameter-entity
Description: Parameter entity XXE — uses % entity for blind detection
Detection: XXE payload "parameter-entity" succeeded — response matches indicator: /root:.*:0:0|\/bin\/(ba)?sh|error|DTD/i
Response status: 403

<!--[if lt IE 7]> <html class="no-js ie6 oldie" lang="en-US"> <![endif]-->

evidencePack.curlCommand:

  -X POST \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/xml' \

<!DOCTYPE foo [
  <!ENTITY % xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">
  %xxe;
]>
<root>test</root>' \
  -L \
  -i \
  'https://cal.com/api/geolocation'
responseIndicators:
Response body contains: <!DOCTYPE html>
<!--[if lt IE 7]> <html class="no-js ie6 oldie" lang="en-US"> <![endif]-->
<!--[if I...

interpretedFindings.title: Directory Traversal / Path Traversal on API Endpoint
interpretedFindings.description: The /api/geolocation endpoint appears vulnerable to directory traversal, potentially allowing an attacker to read arbitrary files from the server filesystem (e.g., /etc/passwd). However, given that AWS WAF and Cloudflare are in place, this finding should be manually verified — it may be a false positive caused by the scanner misinterpreting a generic error or redirect response as file content.
interpretedFindings.impact: If genuine, an attacker could read sensitive server files including credentials, private keys, and configuration files, leading to full server compromise.
reproductionSteps: Send a GET request to https://cal.com/api/../../../etc/passwd
Observe whether the response contains Unix passwd file content (root:.*:0:0 pattern)
Also try: https://cal.com/api/geolocation?path=../../../etc/passwd
Verify manually that the response body actually contains file contents and is not a false positive
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Validate and sanitize all file path inputs server-side. Use path.resolve() and verify the resolved path starts with the expected base directory. Never construct file paths from user-supplied input. Ensure the Next.js API route does not pass any request parameters directly to filesystem operations.

interpretedFindings.title: XXE Injection on API Endpoint
interpretedFindings.description: The /api/geolocation endpoint reportedly accepts XML input and is vulnerable to XML External Entity (XXE) injection via parameter entities. This could allow an attacker to read local files, perform server-side request forgery (SSRF), or cause denial of service. Manual verification is recommended given the WAF/CDN layer in place.
interpretedFindings.impact: Successful XXE exploitation can lead to arbitrary file read (e.g., /etc/passwd, application secrets), internal network scanning via SSRF, and potential denial of service through entity expansion attacks.
reproductionSteps: Send a POST request to https://cal.com/api/geolocation with Content-Type: application/xml
Include payload: <?xml version="1.0"?><!DOCTYPE foo [<!ENTITY % xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd"> %xxe;]><foo/>
Observe whether the response reflects file contents or triggers an out-of-band DNS/HTTP request to an attacker-controlled server
Use a tool like Burp Collaborator for blind XXE detection
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Disable external entity processing in your XML parser. If the endpoint does not need to accept XML, reject non-JSON content types. If XML is required, configure the parser to disallow DOCTYPE declarations and external entities.
interpretedFindings.curlCommand:
curl \
  -X POST \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/xml' \
  -d '<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE foo [
  <!ENTITY % xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">
  %xxe;
]>
<root>test</root>' \
  -L \
  -i \
  'https://cal.com/api/geolocation'

interpretedFindings.title: Exposed Admin-Like Routes Without Verified Authentication
interpretedFindings.description: Multiple paths (/admin, /administrator, /manage, /manager, /management) return HTTP 200 with large HTML bodies. The response snippets show a standard Next.js app shell, suggesting these may be legitimate public-facing routes (e.g., user dashboards or marketing pages) rather than unprotected admin panels. However, if any of these routes expose administrative functionality without authentication, it is a critical issue. Manual verification is required.
interpretedFindings.impact: If these routes expose administrative functionality without authentication, an attacker could gain full control over the application, user data, and system configuration.
reproductionSteps: Visit https://cal.com/admin in a browser while logged out
Check whether the page renders sensitive admin functionality or redirects to login
Repeat for /administrator, /manage, /manager, /management
Inspect the rendered page content for admin controls, user lists, or configuration options
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Ensure all administrative routes are protected by server-side authentication middleware. In Next.js App Router, use middleware.ts to redirect unauthenticated users. Do not rely solely on client-side route guards.

interpretedFindings.title: Sensitive Token Exposed in URL Query Parameter
interpretedFindings.description: A token value is passed as a query parameter in the URL https://cal.com/api/web_experiments/?token=. Query parameters are routinely logged in server access logs, CDN logs, browser history, and may be leaked via the Referer header to third-party scripts. This is particularly concerning given the multiple third-party analytics scripts loaded on the page.
interpretedFindings.impact: Token leakage via logs or Referer headers could allow attackers to hijack sessions, impersonate users, or access experiment configurations. CDN and proxy logs (Cloudflare) will capture these tokens.
reproductionSteps: Load https://cal.com/ and inspect network requests in browser DevTools
Find the request to /api/web_experiments/?token=
Note the token value in the URL
Check whether the same token appears in Referer headers sent to third-party analytics endpoints
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Move sensitive tokens from URL query parameters to HTTP request headers (e.g., Authorization: Bearer <token>) or POST request bodies. If the token must be in a URL (e.g., for GET requests), ensure it is short-lived, single-use, and not a long-lived secret.

interpretedFindings.title: Missing Rate Limiting on Authentication Endpoint
interpretedFindings.description: The /api/auth/session endpoint accepts at least 15 rapid successive requests without returning a 429 Too Many Requests response or any rate-limit headers. This could facilitate brute-force attacks or credential stuffing. Note that AWS WAF may provide some protection at the infrastructure level that is not reflected in response headers.
interpretedFindings.impact: Without rate limiting, attackers can attempt unlimited authentication requests, enabling brute-force password attacks, credential stuffing, and account enumeration at scale.
reproductionSteps: Send 20+ rapid POST requests to https://cal.com/api/auth/session
Observe that all requests return HTTP 200 with no X-RateLimit-* or Retry-After headers
Confirm no 429 response is received
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Implement rate limiting on authentication endpoints using a library like 'next-rate-limit' or 'upstash/ratelimit'. Apply stricter limits to auth endpoints than general API endpoints. Also ensure AWS WAF rate-based rules are configured for this path.

interpretedFindings.title: OAuth State Parameter Not Enforced (CSRF on OAuth Flow)
interpretedFindings.description: The /api/auth/session endpoint responds with HTTP 200 when called with OAuth parameters but without a state parameter. The state parameter is required by OAuth 2.0 (RFC 6749) to prevent CSRF attacks on the authorization flow. However, /api/auth/session is a NextAuth.js session endpoint, not a standard OAuth authorization endpoint — this may be a false positive from the scanner probing the wrong endpoint.
interpretedFindings.impact: If the OAuth flow genuinely lacks state parameter validation, an attacker could trick a user into authorizing a malicious OAuth application, potentially hijacking their account.
reproductionSteps: Initiate an OAuth login flow on cal.com
Intercept the authorization request and remove the state parameter
Observe whether the flow completes successfully without the state parameter
If it does, the CSRF protection is missing
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: NextAuth.js handles state parameter generation automatically for OAuth providers. Ensure you are using an up-to-date version of NextAuth.js and have not disabled CSRF protection. Verify that custom OAuth implementations include state parameter generation and validation.

interpretedFindings.title: Missing Subresource Integrity (SRI) on External Scripts
interpretedFindings.owaspCategory: A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures
interpretedFindings.description: Seven external JavaScript resources are loaded without integrity attributes. These include third-party analytics and advertising scripts from CloudFront, PostHog, and Twitter Ads. If any of these external hosts are compromised, malicious code could be injected and executed in users' browsers without detection.
interpretedFindings.impact: A supply chain attack on any of these third-party CDNs could result in malicious JavaScript executing in all cal.com users' browsers, enabling credential theft, session hijacking, or data exfiltration.
reproductionSteps: View page source of https://cal.com/
Search for <script src= tags pointing to external domains
Verify that none have integrity="sha384-..." attributes
Affected domains include ddwl4m2hdecbv.cloudfront.net, eu-assets.i.posthog.com, and static.ads-twitter.com
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Add integrity and crossorigin attributes to all external script tags. For dynamically loaded or frequently updated third-party scripts (like analytics), consider self-hosting them or using a strict Content Security Policy as a compensating control. Note: SRI is not practical for scripts that update frequently without version pinning.
interpretedFindings.codeExample:
<!-- Generate hash: openssl dgst -sha384 -binary script.js | openssl base64 -A -->
<script
  src="https://eu-assets.i.posthog.com/static/array.js"
  integrity="sha384-<generated-hash-here>"
  crossorigin="anonymous"
></script>

<!-- In Next.js, use the Script component with integrity prop -->
import Script from 'next/script';

<Script
  src="https://eu-assets.i.posthog.com/static/array.js"
  integrity="sha384-<hash>"
  crossOrigin="anonymous"
/>

// Alternatively, strengthen CSP as a compensating control:
// Content-Security-Policy: script-src 'self' https://eu-assets.i.posthog.com 'sha384-...'

interpretedFindings.title: Auth Cookie Missing HttpOnly Flag
interpretedFindings.description: The __Secure-next-auth.callback-url cookie is set without the HttpOnly flag, making it accessible to JavaScript running on the page. While this specific cookie stores a callback URL (not a session token), it can still be read and manipulated by malicious scripts. The __Secure- prefix correctly enforces HTTPS-only transmission.
interpretedFindings.impact: If an XSS vulnerability exists (currently none found), this cookie could be read by attacker-injected JavaScript. An attacker could also manipulate the callback URL to redirect users to malicious sites after authentication.
reproductionSteps: Visit https://cal.com/ and open browser DevTools
Go to Application > Cookies > https://cal.com
Find __Secure-next-auth.callback-url
Confirm HttpOnly column shows false/unchecked
Run in console: document.cookie — verify the cookie value is accessible
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Set the HttpOnly flag on all authentication-related cookies, including callback URL cookies. In NextAuth.js, configure cookie options to include httpOnly: true. Validate the callback URL server-side to prevent open redirect abuse regardless of cookie flags.

topIssues: Manually verify and remediate the directory traversal and XXE findings on /api/geolocation — if confirmed, these represent critical RCE/file-read risks
Verify that /admin, /manage, /manager, /management, /administrator routes enforce server-side authentication — scanner evidence suggests these may be legitimate public routes but must be confirmed
Move the web_experiments API token from URL query parameters to Authorization headers to prevent token leakage via server logs and Referer headers
